infoSection.infoPara1: This resource is designed to assist with professional consultations, not replace them.

modelSections.buttonLabel: What will this app do for me?
modelSections.modalTitle: What will this app do for me?
modelSections.para1: This app helps you organise what you already see.
modelSections.para2: Clinicians don’t look for one behaviour or moment. They look for patterns over time across a small number of areas. These areas come from the DSM-5 criteria used in UK assessments. They help professionals understand how a child experiences the world. This app will help you understand the DSM –5 areas, identify what works for your child and gather examples easily for any assessments. By identifying patterns, we can start to understand what different behaviours are communicating. This understanding can help gather evidence for diagnosis, highlight support needs in school and decide on adjustments to help your child.

modelSections.para1: You only need to notice what happens, where, and how it affects your child. The journaling section lets you make a record, in your own words. You can also chose to tag the journal entry under one of the following categories and the impact it had on your child.
modelSections.para2: When you want to look back for patterns or specific moments, just head to the Review page where you can sort and filter to make findings things easy.

modelSections.para1: TBC

TBC
TBC

How does your child communicate such as sharing interests, responding to others, and taking turns in conversation. This section also includes nonverbal communication, incorporating how eye contact, facial expression, tone of voice, and gestures are used and understood.

Repetitive movements or speech. Repeated actions, sounds, phrases, or patterns of play that may be calming or regulating. Strong preferences for routines, predictability, or familiar ways of doing things. Intense interests that may be unusual in intensity or take up a lot of time and attention. These patterns often help a child feel safe and organised.

How a child develops, understands, and maintains relationships with peers and adults. Differences here don’t mean a lack of care or empathy — they reflect different ways of connecting. This area also covers interacting with the wider social world including interaction with strangers and groups.

This area allows you to capture when you child has either struggled to regulate or has coped well with a situation. Capturing meltdowns and avoided-meltdowns can be a powerful tool in understanding how to help you child.

Being more or less sensitive to sounds, lights, textures, movement, pain, temperature, or other sensory input. Sensory needs can change depending on stress, tiredness, or environment. Difficulty with toileting or other internal sensory processing.

This area is about the thinking skills that help us plan, decide what to do, and follow things through including working memory, response inhibition (keeping things in), task initiation, sustained attention, planning and time management.

If sensory supports are new to you, it’s okay to start small and be curious. There’s no “right” way to use these tools — some people may love them, others may not, and preferences can change over time. A good first step is observing how your child already interacts with their environment and their behaviour. Before investing you can potentially visit spaces with supports such as play spaces or local charities to see if they would benefit your child.
Sensory supports can help people feel comfortable, calm, and able to engage in daily life. They respect different sensory needs and preferences rather than trying to change who a child is.
modelSections.para2: Common examples include ear defenders for loud environments, chewy jewellery for oral sensory input, and fidget toys that support focus and regulation. What’s useful will vary for each child.

modelSections.para1: TBC

Communication supports help people express themselves, understand others, and take part in everyday interactions in ways that work for them. They recognise that communication can take many forms — not just spoken words — and support autonomy, dignity, and connection.
modelSections.para2: Examples include AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) such as picture‑based systems, communication boards, or speech apps; emotion cards to help identify and express feelings; and flash cards or visual supports to make routines, choices, and expectations clearer. These tools can reduce frustration, build confidence, and support meaningful communication across home, school, and community settings.

modelSections.para1: TBC

Emotional regulation supports are tools and activities that help people manage their energy, emotions, and sensory experiences in ways that feel safe and supportive to them. They recognise that bodies regulate differently, and that movement, pressure, or proprioceptive input can be helpful — not distracting.
modelSections.para2: Examples include weighted vests that provide calming deep pressure, trampolines for releasing excess energy, hammocks or swings for rhythmic movement, and body socks that offer resistance and full‑body sensory input. Used respectfully and by choice, these supports can help people feel more grounded, organised, and ready to engage.

modelSections.para1: TBC

Executive functioning supports are tools that help people organise their time, attention, and tasks in ways that feel clearer and less overwhelming. They support planning, transitions, memory, and follow‑through, recognising that these skills develop differently for everyone.
modelSections.para2: Examples include timers to make time visible, visual timetables to show what’s happening across the day, and “now and next” boards to support understanding of immediate transitions. These supports can reduce anxiety, support independence, and make expectations more predictable at home and school.
